subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Electrochemistry connects thermodynamics to electron transfer, linking $\Delta G^\circ$, $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$, and $K$ in a unified framework.
core_concepts: Galvanic (voltaic) cells produce electricity from spontaneous redox reactions ($\Delta G^\circ < 0$, $E^\circ > 0$); electrolytic cells use electricity to drive non-spontaneous reactions.
Anode = oxidation (AN OX); Cathode = reduction (RED CAT); electrons flow from anode to cathode through the wire.
Cell potential: $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$; species with higher reduction potential preferentially gets reduced.
The master relationship: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{\text{cell}} = -RT\ln K$; connects all three thermodynamic quantities.
The Nernst equation: $E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$ at 25°C; accounts for non-standard concentrations.
Faraday's law of electrolysis: moles of product = $\frac{It}{nF}$; used to calculate mass deposited or time needed.
Corrosion is an electrochemical process; cathodic protection (sacrificial anode) prevents oxidation by connecting a more reactive metal.

key_terms.definition: Electrochemical cell converting spontaneous chemical energy to electrical energy; $E^\circ > 0$, $\Delta G^\circ < 0$
Cell requiring electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous reaction; used in electroplating and electrolysis
Electrode where oxidation occurs; negative in galvanic cell, positive in electrolytic cell
Electrode where reduction occurs; positive in galvanic cell, negative in electrolytic cell
Voltage for a half-reaction under standard conditions relative to SHE (0 V); higher = stronger oxidizing agent
$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0592}{n}\log Q$; adjusts cell potential for non-standard concentrations at 25°C
$96{,}485$ C/mol; charge per mole of electrons

exam_tip: The three quantities $\Delta G^\circ$, $E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$, and $K$ are all interconnected — memorize $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ = -RT\ln K$ so you can convert between any two.
